title: Lifestyle Design
subtitle: Intentional choices, habits, and quality of each day
description: Lifestyle is not what happens to you by default. It is the sum of intentional decisions about how you spend your time, attention, and energy. This checklist is about designing a life you don't want to escape from on vacation.

items.title: Eudaimonia vs. Hedonia
items.text: Hedonia is the pursuit of pleasure in the here and now (tasty food, Netflix). Eudaimonia is happiness derived from realizing potential and meaningful activity. Pleasure fades quickly; meaning provides lasting life satisfaction.
items.tip: At the end of the day, ask: 'What brought me genuine satisfaction today, not just entertainment?'

items.title: Four Thousand Weeks
items.text: The average human lifespan is about 4000 weeks. Accepting the finitude of time paradoxically frees you. You cannot do everything, so you must consciously choose what you will miss out on. Choice is always a rejection of alternatives.
items.tip: Make a list of what you consciously give up this year to make room for what matters most.

items.title: Habits as Identity
items.text: Every action is a vote for the type of person you are becoming. Brushing teeth, going for a run, reading — these are not just tasks, they are affirmations of identity. The focus shifts from 'I want to run a marathon' to 'I am a runner'.
items.tip: Start with a two-minute version of the habit. Just putting on your sneakers is enough to become 'someone who runs'.

items.title: Digital Minimalism
items.text: Technology is an excellent servant but a terrible master. Spending 3-4 hours a day on your phone is not rest, it's dopamine system depletion. Time alone without incoming stimuli is necessary for self-reflection and creativity.
items.tip: Schedule a 'digital Sabbath' — 24 hours a week without internet and screens.

items.title: Essentialism
items.text: Less but better. The essentialist constantly asks: 'Is this activity absolutely necessary?' Saying no to good opportunities in favor of one great one is the highest form of discipline.
items.tip: If the answer to an offer isn't 'HELL YES!', it's 'No'.

items.title: Mindfulness of the Ordinary
items.source: Thich Nhat Hanh — The Miracle of Mindfulness
items.text: We spend life waiting for the 'real' moment (vacation, weekend), missing life itself. The ability to find interest and beauty in routine (the taste of tea, the walk to the subway) is a trainable skill.
items.tip: Once a day, perform one routine action (washing dishes, brushing teeth) with full attention to the sensations.

items.title: The Ideal Day
items.text: Visualize not abstract 'success', but a specific ideal Tuesday in 5 years. What time do you wake up? What do you eat for breakfast? Where do you work? What do you do in the evening? This exercise provides a much more accurate compass for decisions than financial goals.
items.tip: Describe your ideal ordinary day in the present tense, as if it has already arrived.

items.title: Enough
items.source: Vicki Robin — Your Money or Your Life
items.text: The currency of life is time and energy. Every purchase is exchanging hours of your life for an item. The point of 'Enough' is when additional money stops bringing proportional happiness and starts creating extra noise.
items.tip: Calculate your real hourly wage, including commute and work preparation time.

items.title: Boredom as a Catalyst
items.source: Sandie Mann — The Science of Boredom
items.text: Modern humans have forgotten how to be bored. At the first sign of emptiness, the hand reaches for the phone. But it is in a state of boredom that the brain begins to wander and create new neural connections, giving rise to creative ideas and self-awareness.
items.tip: Stand in line or ride the elevator without your phone. Observe the thoughts that come.

items.title: Weak Ties
items.source: Mark Granovetter — The Strength of Weak Ties
items.text: Close friends provide support, but new opportunities (jobs, ideas) more often come through 'weak ties' — acquaintances you rarely see. Maintaining a wide but not deep network of contacts increases the 'surface area of luck'.
items.tip: Once a month, write to an old acquaintance you haven't spoken to in a while, just to see how they're doing.

quiz.q: How does eudaimonia differ from hedonia?
a: Eudaimonia is happiness from meaning and development

quiz.q: What is 'Essentialism' according to Greg McKeown?
Why are 'weak ties' important for opportunities?
What does the 'Ideal Ordinary Day' exercise propose?
a: Understand true values through daily life details

quiz.q: Why is boredom beneficial for the brain?
a: Activates the mind-wandering mode and creativity
